goal: 10-minute live lecture teaching students to read a USD stage via run_stage_analysis on a chosen scene.
thoughts: Pedagogic sweet spot: 20-80 prims. Too small = nothing to show, too big = unreadable. A tabletop manipulation scene (robot + sensors + lights + table + objects) hits ~30-50. Three teachable moments: (1) Xform parenting — move parent, children follow; (2) API schemas — same prim type, different physics; (3) references — same asset, multiple instances. Exercise: add cube, re-run, diff.
code:
# Pick the right-sized scene
list_scene_templates()
load_scene_template(template_name="<TABLETOP_MANIPULATION_STARTER>")

# Live-demo the tool students will use
run_stage_analysis()

# Teachable moment 1: all Franka parts under /World/Franka Xform
#   → move parent, children follow. Demo: set_attribute on /World/Franka’s translate.
# Teachable moment 2: /World/Franka has PhysicsArticulationRootAPI,
#   /World/Table has PhysicsCollisionAPI — same prim type, different behaviors.
# Teachable moment 3: /World/Cup is a Reference to cup.usd; add another reference
#   and both update if the source changes.

# Student exercise:
add_reference(prim_path="/World/ExerciseCube", reference_path="<CUBE_USD>")
run_stage_analysis()  # students diff output — what changed?

# Depth for "what is Xform?": "A coordinate-frame container. Children inherit
# its transform." Stop there unless asked.
failure_modes: Picking a 500-prim warehouse — analysis output scrolls off screen
Picking an empty scene — no hierarchy to teach
Jargon-dumping (prim, schema, xformOp) without one-line definitions
Skipping the diff exercise — students do not feel the tool's usefulness
